turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: Thanks for hopping on, Daniel. To start, can you walk me through the first day you set up Maple for your team?

turns.speaker: Daniel
turns.text: Sure. Honestly the very first hour was rough. Signing up was fine, but the moment I tried to connect our business bank account it just spun and then threw a generic error.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: What did the error say, if you remember?

turns.speaker: Daniel
turns.text: Something like 'We couldn't reach your institution, try again later.' No explanation. I tried four times across two days before it finally connected. I almost gave up and assumed the product was broken.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: That sounds frustrating. Was there any guidance during that flow?

turns.speaker: Daniel
turns.text: Not really. There was no checklist telling me what to do first. I would have loved a simple 'set up in three steps' kind of wizard. Instead I was clicking around the dashboard trying to figure out the right order.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: Once you got past the bank connection, how did the rest of setup go?

turns.speaker: Daniel
turns.text: Inviting teammates was smooth, that part I liked. And categorizing the first batch of transactions was actually kind of satisfying. But getting there took two days when it should have taken twenty minutes.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: If you could change one thing about that first experience, what would it be?

turns.speaker: Daniel
turns.text: A guided onboarding checklist that tells me the order to do things in, and a much clearer error when the bank link fails. Tell me whether it's my bank, my credentials, or your side. The mystery is what made me lose trust early.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: Did that early friction affect how your team sees the product now?

turns.speaker: Daniel
turns.text: We use it daily now and it's fine, but two of my teammates still think of Maple as 'the thing that was a pain to set up.' First impressions really stuck.
